items.q: Why do meanders migrate across a floodplain?
items.correct: Faster water on the outside of the bend causes erosion (river cliff); slower water on the inside causes deposition (slip-off slope) — over time the channel shifts laterally.
items.misconception: Meanders form because rivers flow around obstacles like rocks.
items.random_wrong: Meanders form because the river is going downhill very steeply.
items.why: Meanders form on gentle gradients, not steep ones, and self-organise rather than dodging obstacles. Students project an intuitive 'water goes around things' model onto a fluid dynamic process.

items.q: Why is the Brandt Line considered outdated?
items.correct: Drawn in 1980, it predates the rise of NICs (China, India, Brazil) and globalisation — countries like South Korea, Singapore and Chile no longer fit neatly on the 'poor south' side.
items.misconception: The Brandt Line still accurately divides rich and poor countries today.
items.random_wrong: The Brandt Line is the boundary between the Atlantic and Pacific oceans.
items.why: The world has changed: BRICs have grown, some HICs have stagnated. A simple north-south divide cannot capture this. Many geographers prefer the term 'Global North/Global South' loosely or reject the binary altogether.

items.q: What is the difference between the focus and epicentre of an earthquake?
items.correct: The focus (hypocentre) is the underground point where the earthquake originates; the epicentre is the point on the surface directly above the focus.
items.misconception: The focus and epicentre are the same point.
items.random_wrong: The focus is at the centre of the Earth and the epicentre is on the surface.
items.why: Students treat the two terms as synonymous or place the focus deep at the Earth's core. The focus is just below the epicentre, typically only a few to tens of kilometres deep.

items.q: Which transport project opened in London in 2022?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Crossrail (Elizabeth Line). “High Speed 1” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.
Correct answer: City of London. “Camden” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Roughly how many languages are spoken in London?
items.why: The real figure is 300. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
